name: GPflow (Gaussian Processes via TensorFlow)
pricing_notes: Open source (Apache-2.0); GPflow team / James Hensman

performance.notes: TF2/eager mode; GPU support; sparse GP (SVGP) scales to large datasets

agent_readiness.notes: Gaussian Process library on TensorFlow 2. import gpflow. kernel = gpflow.kernels.SquaredExponential(). m = gpflow.models.GPR((X_train, Y_train), kernel=kernel). opt = gpflow.optimizers.Scipy(). opt.minimize(m.training_loss, m.trainable_variables). mean, var = m.predict_y(X_test). SVGP for large data: m = gpflow.models.SVGP(kernel, gpflow.likelihoods.Gaussian(), inducing_variable=Z). gpflow.utilities.print_summary(m). Custom kernels via TensorFlow. Heteroskedastic likelihoods. Sparse approximations. Multi-output GPs. Integrate with TFP. gpflow.config for float64 default.

gotchas: TensorFlow dependency — heavier than GPyTorch for PyTorch users
Float64 required for numerical stability — set with gpflow.config.set_default_float()
GPyTorch often preferred for deep GP and PyTorch integration
Sparse GP (SVGP) inducing point selection is non-trivial
TF2 eager mode makes debugging easier but still TF abstraction
best_when: Gaussian process regression and classification on TensorFlow
Sparse GP (SVGP) for large-scale datasets with inducing points
Custom kernel and likelihood development with TF autodiff
When TF2/Keras ecosystem is already in use
avoid_when: Simple GP regression (use scikit-learn GaussianProcessRegressor for quick use)
summary: GPflow provides Gaussian processes on TensorFlow 2. GPR and SVGP models. kernel = SquaredExponential(); m = GPR((X, Y), kernel). Scipy optimizer. predict_y(X_test). Sparse GP with inducing points. Custom kernels via TF. Multi-output GPs. Heteroskedastic likelihoods. Apache-2.0 by GPflow Team. Best for TF-native GP regression and sparse GP at scale. Float64 required. GPyTorch preferred for PyTorch projects.
